title: Question 5
text:
Finally, we compare the run time between parLapply, foreach and replicate for Question 4.

library(parallel)
library(doParallel)
library(foreach)


set.seed(1234)

# Generate the random vectors
random_vectors <- as.list(replicate(3, rnorm(5), simplify = FALSE))

# Set up parallel backend
num_cores <- detectCores(logical = FALSE)
cl <- makeCluster(min(2, num_cores))
registerDoParallel(cl)

# 1. Using parLapply
time_parLapply <- system.time({
  res_parLapply <- parLapply(cl, random_vectors, max)
})

# 2. Using foreach with %dopar% (NO artificial sleep!)
time_foreach <- system.time({
  res_foreach <- foreach(v = random_vectors, .combine = c) %dopar% {
    max(v)
  }
})

# 3. Using replicate (NOT sapply)
# replicate() repeatedly evaluates an expression
time_replicate <- system.time({
  # Approach A: If you want to apply max to each vector
  res_replicate <- replicate(3, {
    # This is awkward - replicate isn't designed for this!
    # Better to use sapply/lapply for this task
    max(rnorm(5))  # But this generates NEW data each time!
  })
})

# Better: Use lapply for the actual task, since replicate isn't appropriate
# The question might mean "compare with non-parallel version"
time_serial <- system.time({
  res_serial <- lapply(random_vectors, max)
})

# Clean up
stopCluster(cl)

# Print results
cat("parLapply time:\n")

parLapply time:

print(time_parLapply)

   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.02 

cat("\nforeach time:\n") 


foreach time:

print(time_foreach)

   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.07 

cat("\nSerial lapply time (for comparison):\n")


Serial lapply time (for comparison):

print(time_serial)

   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0 

# Compare results (should all be the same)
cat("\nResults:\n")


Results:

cat("parLapply:", unlist(res_parLapply), "\n")

parLapply: 1.084441 0.5060559 0.9594941 

cat("foreach:", res_foreach, "\n")

foreach: 1.084441 0.5060559 0.9594941 

cat("serial:", unlist(res_serial), "\n")

serial: 1.084441 0.5060559 0.9594941

title: Question 3
text:
We now turn to estimating the coverage of a percentile bootstrap confidence interval for a sample size of 50 sampled from an exponential distribution of mean 1.

library(boot)

Warning: package 'boot' was built under R version 4.4.3



set.seed(2025)  # For reproducibility

true_mean <- 1
n <- 50              # Sample size
B <- 1000            # Number of bootstrap resamples
n_sim <- 1000        # Number of simulations to check coverage

# Bootstrap function
boot_mean <- function(data, indices) {
  mean(data[indices])
}

# Track coverage
cover <- 0

for (i in 1:n_sim) {
  # Generate fresh sample from Exponential(1)
  sample_data <- rexp(n, rate = 1)
  
  # Perform bootstrap
  boot_result <- boot(sample_data, boot_mean, R = B)
  
  # Get percentile bootstrap CI
  ci <- boot.ci(boot_result, type = "perc")$percent[4:5]
  
  # Check if true mean is in CI
  if (!is.null(ci) && true_mean >= ci[1] && true_mean <= ci[2]) {
    cover <- cover + 1
  }
}

# Calculate coverage probability
coverage_prob <- cover / n_sim
cat("Coverage probability:", coverage_prob, "\n")

Coverage probability: 0.94 

cat("Expected 95% CI coverage: 0.95\n")

Expected 95% CI coverage: 0.95

cat("Difference:", abs(coverage_prob - 0.95))

Difference: 0.01

title: Question 1
text:
In this question we generate a random sample from an exponential distribution with a mean of 1 using a foreach loop repeated a 100 times.

mean_variance <- function(x){
  c(mean(x), var(x))
}

set.seed(2025)
results <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = rbind) %do% {
  samples <- rexp(5000, rate = 1)
  mean_variance(samples)  # Fixed variable name
}

# Add column names for clarity
colnames(results) <- c("Mean", "Variance")
head(results)

              Mean  Variance
result.1 1.0059263 1.0192163
result.2 1.0069620 0.9881573
result.3 0.9950264 1.0175815
result.4 0.9877392 0.9396905
result.5 1.0012039 1.0183514
result.6 0.9919160 0.9610271

# Check results
cat("Average of means:", mean(results[,1]), "(true mean = 1)\n")

Average of means: 0.9988383 (true mean = 1)

cat("Average of variances:", mean(results[,2]), "(true variance = 1)\n")

Average of variances: 0.9968416 (true variance = 1)

title: Question2
text:
We use doParallel and foreach to bootstrap the median for the galaxies data in the MASS library with the purpose of comparing times between the two approaches.

# Set up cluster
cl <- makeCluster(4)
registerDoParallel(cl)

B <- 1000  # Number of bootstrap samples

# METHOD 1: PARALLEL
time_individual <- system.time({
  res_parallel <- foreach(i = 1:B, 
                         .combine = c, 
                         .packages = "MASS",
                         .export = "galaxies") %dopar% {
    median(sample(galaxies, replace = TRUE))
  }
})
cat("Time elapsed:", time_individual["elapsed"], "seconds\n")

Time elapsed: 1.08 seconds

cat("First few results:", head(res_parallel), "\n\n")

First few results: 21596.5 20179 21701 20875 21183.5 20422 

# METHOD 2: PARALLEL BUT CHUNKED
chunk_size <- 100
num_chunks <- B / chunk_size

time_chunked <- system.time({
  res_chunked <- foreach(chunk = 1:num_chunks, 
                        .combine = c,
                        .packages = "MASS",
                        .export = "galaxies") %dopar% {
    
    # Process chunk_size bootstrap samples in ONE worker
    chunk_results <- replicate(chunk_size, {
      median(sample(galaxies, replace = TRUE))
    })
    
    return(chunk_results)
  }
})
cat("Time elapsed:", time_chunked["elapsed"], "seconds\n")

Time elapsed: 0.07 seconds

cat("First few results:", head(res_chunked), "\n\n")

First few results: 21814 20795 20318 20808 20175 20179 

# METHOD 3: SERIAL (BASELINE)
time_serial <- system.time({
  res_serial <- foreach(i = 1:B, 
                       .combine = c, 
                       .packages = "MASS") %do% {
    median(sample(galaxies, replace = TRUE))
  }
})
cat("Time elapsed:", time_serial["elapsed"], "seconds\n")

Time elapsed: 0.44 seconds

cat("First few results:", head(res_serial), "\n\n")

First few results: 19989 20808 20875 20179 20833.5 20629 

# Stop the cluster
stopCluster(cl)

# Create comparison table
comparison <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Parallel (individual)", "Parallel (chunked)", "Serial (baseline)"),
  Time_sec = c(time_individual["elapsed"], 
               time_chunked["elapsed"], 
               time_serial["elapsed"])
)

# Calculate speedup relative to serial
comparison$Speedup_vs_serial <- round(comparison$Time_sec[3] / comparison$Time_sec, 2)
comparison$Efficiency <- round(comparison$Speedup_vs_serial / 4 * 100, 1)  # 4 cores

print(comparison)

                 Method Time_sec Speedup_vs_serial Efficiency
1 Parallel (individual)     1.08              0.41       10.2
2    Parallel (chunked)     0.07              6.29      157.2
3     Serial (baseline)     0.44              1.00       25.0

title: Question 4
text:
We utilise the foreach and inorm functions to iterate over 3 vectors, each containing 5 random variables, additionally, we find the largest value in each vector and print out these large values.

library(foreach)
library(doParallel)
library(iterators)


set.seed(1234)

# Set up parallel backend
cl <- makeCluster(2)  # even though the question doesn't specify, 2 is a safe value to use
registerDoParallel(cl)

# Iterate over 3 vectors, each with 5 random normals
# Find the largest value in each vector
result <- foreach(x = irnorm(5, count = 3), .combine = c) %dopar% {
  max(x)
}

# Clean up
stopCluster(cl)

# Print the largest values
print(result)

[1] 1.0844412 0.5060559 0.9594941
